findings.description: The 'index' header-position map is built but never used; parsing relies on hardcoded positions row[0..3], so columns in a non-canonical order are mapped to the wrong fields.
Row access row[0..3] never checks len(row), so a short/malformed row raises an uncaught IndexError that aborts the entire parse.
int(row[0]) and float(row[2]) raise uncaught ValueError on non-numeric data, crashing parse instead of skipping or reporting the bad row.
open(path).readlines() leaks the file handle (never closed) and reads the whole file into memory just to count lines.
count_rows returns -1 for an empty file and miscounts when a trailing blank line exists or CSV fields contain quoted embedded newlines (line count != row count).
list(reader) materializes the entire CSV in memory; stream_valid claims to stream but calls parse, defeating streaming for large uploads.
User-controlled name/email values are written to CSV without neutralizing leading =,+,-,@, enabling CSV/formula injection when the export is opened in a spreadsheet.
os.path.basename only partially mitigates path traversal; a caller can still overwrite any existing file in export_dir by collision, and unusual basenames are not validated.
os.makedirs side effect in __init__ can raise PermissionError at construction time for the default /var/data/clean path.
parse performs no email-format validation even though stream_valid relies on a weak '@' substring check downstream.
export opens dest with mode 'w' and no explicit permissions; cleaned data possibly containing PII (emails) may be written world-readable depending on umask.
